quote: Yeah. But it's my son. I guess so.
stewart_read: The customer identifies a son on the title. Carter acknowledged it ('Your son. I make it a good one. Yeah. Cool.') and moved immediately to the credit question. The son was never included in the appointment. Per the schema, a co-decision-maker on title must be present at the close — booking without them is structurally fragile.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter may have assessed the son as a passive title-holder rather than an active decision-maker, and chose not to complicate the booking. No evidence supports this — the customer's phrasing ('I guess so') suggests uncertainty about the son's role, which is precisely the moment to clarify and include.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play the clip at 01:32 and ask Carter: 'What did you do with the son?' Walk through the schema protocol — the correct move is 'Let's make sure your son is on the call with us. What time works for both of you?' The closer is now walking into a home visit where a title-holder may not be present.

quote: Yeah. Did you put in a request to get some information on solar?
stewart_read: Carter never introduced himself as being with Ion Solar, never used the Tesla framing available for legitimacy, and never asked what interested the customer in solar (verify.interest_in_solar). The call opened as a callback confirmation and immediately became a data-collection exercise. The customer has no emotional reason to show up to the appointment.
alternative_hypothesis: The customer called back after missing Ion's outbound — legitimacy may have been partially established by the prior missed call. This reduces (but does not eliminate) the intro gap. The interest-in-solar miss is unaffected by this context.
coaching_implication: Show Carter the intros.standard template and the verify.interest_in_solar coaching note. The question 'What interested you in solar?' is the emotional anchor for the entire pitch — skipping it means the closer has no bill-pain context and no curiosity hook to build on. Even on a callback, 15 seconds of framing changes the call's energy.

stewart_read: Carter confirmed the utility company and immediately pivoted to bill format (mail vs online) and bill collection. The average bill was never asked verbally. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, the bill must be captured AND emotionally flipped — but here the verbal anchor ('Do you know how much you're paying on average?') was skipped entirely before the bill-collection slog began. Carter has no bill-pain number to work with even as a conversational anchor.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may have intended to extract the bill figure from the uploaded document rather than asking verbally — a reasonable efficiency move if the bill arrives cleanly. However, the bill-flip opportunity (aligning the customer emotionally against the utility) requires the customer to say the number out loud. A document alone doesn't create that moment.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, distinguish between bill capture (document) and bill anchor (verbal). The verbal ask — 'Do you know roughly what you're paying Dominion each month?' — takes 10 seconds and creates the emotional hook. The document is for the closer's design; the verbal number is for the setter's pitch. Carter skipped the pitch tool.

quote: K. Awesome. Yeah. So just for townhomes, you do need to own your own section of the roof and have your own electric meter. Is that the case for you?
stewart_read: Carter correctly identified the townhome ownership question but never followed up with roof type or age — a hard DQ condition per the schema. On a townhome, roof age is particularly relevant because the customer may not control the full roof structure. The question was never asked at any point in the call.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may have planned to let the closer assess roof condition on-site during the in-person visit. For an in-person appointment this is less catastrophic than for a phone sit — but it still means the closer walks in without knowing whether a re-roof conversation is needed, and the roofing cross-sell signal was never opened.
coaching_implication: Add roof type/age to Carter's verify checklist as a non-negotiable. The question takes 10 seconds: 'And how old is the roof on your section?' If the answer triggers a re-roof conversation, that's a cross-sell routing opportunity (cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned), not a dead end.

quote: is there something you can do over the phone or online, you know, over Zoom?
stewart_read: The customer stated a clear preference for phone or Zoom before Carter offered any options. Carter echo-confirmed the preference, then pivoted to in-person on the basis of company proximity and preference alone. The customer accepted, but the acceptance was compliance-based — no value bridge was made for why in-person serves the customer better. The booking is structurally fragile: a customer who preferred remote and was talked into in-person is a no-show candidate.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter may have known that in-person closes at a higher rate and pushed accordingly. This is a legitimate floor strategy. The failure is not the push itself — it's the absence of a customer-facing reason. 'We're close by and it's easier to walk through the design together on your screen' would have been a 10-second value bridge that converts compliance into buy-in.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Carter: 'Why did you push for in-person?' If the answer is 'it closes better,' that's correct instinct — but the customer needs a reason that serves them, not the company. Coach the value bridge: the design is visual, walking through it together takes 40 minutes and saves back-and-forth. One sentence. The push is fine; the naked company-preference framing is the gap.

quote: And I I'm okay just to wait, so take your time. No rush.
stewart_read: Carter's patience during the bill-collection slog is genuine and appropriate — the customer was navigating a spam filter and a deleted email, and Carter stayed calm and helpful. This is a small trust-building moment that kept the call from derailing. Worth noting as a positive signal in an otherwise mechanical call.
coaching_implication: Acknowledge this in the 1-on-1 as a positive. Carter's composure during the bill friction kept the customer engaged. The coaching gap is not patience — it's that the 4+ minutes of bill friction was never converted into a rapport moment or a curiosity hook. The wait time was dead air that could have been used to ask about the customer's interest in solar.

quote: Awesome. And then we'll go ahead and schedule an appointment.
stewart_read: Carter moved directly from bill receipt to scheduling without any curiosity creation, bill-pain acknowledgment, or value bridge. The button-up itself is missing the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake (schema: button_up.is_that_fair_handshake) and the text-followup promise (button_up.text_followup_promise). The appointment was confirmed but without the micro-commitments that predict show rate.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, walk through the button_up checklist. Two specific gaps: (1) 'Is that fair?' — the verbal handshake that converts a scheduled time into a commitment. (2) The text-followup promise — 'I'll send you a text with the details after we hang up.' Both take under 10 seconds and materially affect show rate. Carter completed neither.

stewart_read: The customer's close is cooperative but flat — 'Sounds good' with no restatement of interest, no question about what to expect, no energy. This is the direct consequence of Carter never asking what interested the customer in solar. The closer walks into an in-person appointment with a customer who agreed to show up but has no articulated reason to care. The enthusiasm signal here is its absence — the customer had enough interest to call back after a missed outbound, which is a qualified signal Carter never surfaced or amplified.
coaching_implication: Use this close as the anchor for the interest-in-solar coaching conversation. The customer called back — that's a warm signal. Carter never asked 'What got you curious about solar?' A 60-second answer to that question would have given Carter a tieback for the text follow-up, a hook for the closer's opening, and a reason for the customer to show up. The call-back behavior is the enthusiasm signal; Carter walked past it entirely.
